intro: react-native-iap is free and open source while App Radar runs on paid subscription pricing. React-native-iap: Open-source React Native in-app purchase library conforming to OpenIAP spec, using Nitro Modules for native bridging. App Radar: ASO and App Store management platform with keyword tracking, metadata editor, review management, and competitor analysis. React-native-iap fits react Native teams that want to own IAP and validation in-house. App Radar fits mobile — in practice, mobile apps with subscriptions. On our rubric react-native-iap scores 4/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency, while App Radar scores 3/5 and 3/5. The honest trade-off: react-native-iap's main drawback — Library, not a managed subscription service; App Radar's — Free apps without monetization. One point in react-native-iap's favour: Open-source RN IAP aligned with OpenIAP. React-native-iap is explicitly not the right pick for teams that prefer a managed subscription backend. App Radar is not aimed at free apps without monetization. Another react-native-iap advantage: Uses Nitro Modules for modern native bridging. Another App Radar advantage: Paywall A/B testing.
quick_take: react-native-iap is for react Native teams; App Radar is for mobile; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose react-native-iap if react Native teams that want to own IAP and validation in-house, and if you want to avoid recurring license costs and are willing to self-host.
verdict_b: Choose App Radar if your work looks like mobile apps with subscriptions, and if you'd rather pay for a managed service than run infrastructure yourself.

faq.q: Is react-native-iap cheaper than App Radar?
faq.a: Yes on list price: react-native-iap is free and open source, while App Radar runs on paid subscription pricing. Keep in mind that react-native-iap shifts costs to hosting, ops, and engineering time. Total cost of ownership can flip depending on your team's capacity.

faq.q: Can I migrate from react-native-iap to App Radar?
faq.a: Migrating out of react-native-iap is straightforward — it's rated low lock-in (OSS IAP library). The harder question is how deeply you commit to App Radar afterward, since it sits at medium lock-in (ASO data exportable). Plan the forward cost, not just the exit.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, react-native-iap or App Radar?
faq.a: react-native-iap scores higher in our rubric (4/5 vs 3/5 for App Radar). Transparency is 5/5 for react-native-iap and 3/5 for App Radar. DX scores are rubric-based, not benchmarks, so evaluate against your own toolchain.

faq.q: Is App Radar a good alternative to react-native-iap?
faq.a: They sit in the same category, so yes — App Radar is a plausible alternative for many react-native-iap use cases. It fits best when mobile. Skip it if free apps without monetization.
